posts.caption:
#Dior has been making some excellent red carpet looks recently and I can’t help but notice @jonathan.anderson’s revival of the Victorian bustle.
The bustle emerged in the late 19th century as a padded or structured undergarment worn beneath skirts to create volume at the back of the body, emphasizing a narrow waist. They relied on layers of fabric, pleating, and internal frameworks. Aptly linking #ChristianDior’s 1947 “New Look,” emphasising a cinched waist and structured hips.
#JonathanAnderson’s reinterpretation focuses on sculptural volume, creating shapes through draped panels, bows, or layered fabrics, creating extremely sculptural and romantic silhouettes. We’ve seen this in gowns, skirts, tailoring, and shorts, featuring pleated or structured volumes attached to the back of garments, expanding the concept of the bustle beyond traditional #HauteCouture.

posts.caption:
Before Al #MarcJacobs was already building theatrical universes for #LouisVuitton, with immense attention to detail and thousands of human hands.
Like Spring 2013, when models came down in pairs on synchronized escalators. Or Fall 2011, the carousel set was a dreamlike pastel fairytale. Fall 2012 perhaps the grandest, when a steam train arrived on the runway and models stepped off, refrecning the house’s heritage in luxury luggage and travel.
That’s the thing people miss when they talk about Al “replacing” creativity. Marc’s sets weren’t impressive just because they were big, they were specifically responding to culture, heritage and mood something only a human brain could understand and create.
What was your favourite set?

posts.caption:
First introduced in the 1940s by Marchese #EmilioPucci, the Marmo print became a symbol of the jet-set lifestyle, worn by icons like #JackieKennedy and #MarilynMonroe. Now a defining print of summer, @camillemiceli has reignited it through a modern evolved revival.
#Pucci’s annual cruise shows see a carefully curated group of online “it-girls” who effectively act as ambassadors a lifestyle, wearing Pucci throughout and documenting every moment. The strategy taps into the “Euro summer” obsession - Mediterranean luxury - with Pucci positioning itself  at the center of it.
The “Pucci-girl summer” embodies carefree glamour and joyful dressing—a welcome palette cleanser after years of quiet luxury and old money.
Getty Images / Irving Penn for Vogue / Bert Stern for Vogue / Pucci

posts.caption:
#OliviaRodrigo just dropped her new music video, alongside her #Chloe pre fall dress by @chemena and I couldn’t help but notice a clear homage to #JaneBirkin, a version of her 1975 Loris Azzaro dress from Catherine et Cie.
Referencing Jane Birkin (a figure so synonymous with effortless Franco-British cool) within the setting of #Versailles, (a symbol of excess and spectacle) @petrafcollins ’ visual language perfectly captures the spirit of 1970s liberation and femininity that Birkin embodied.
Styled by @chloeandchenelle

posts.caption:
#KimJones has created some of the most incredible runway show sets during his seven years at #Dior Men, and my all-time favourite has to be Spring 2024. Inspired by #ChristianDior’s lifelong love of gardens, @villaeugenie designed a futuristic “mechanical garden.”
The set combined elements of a laboratory and a spaceship, featuring a glowing, grid-like floor made of hidden panels. Models rose up from beneath the runway on concealed platforms, like flowers growing (complete with floral hats by @stephenjonesmillinery )
The clothes were rich in texture and decoration, including jewels, cannage, and the occasional leopard print. While much of the palette remained classic, there were flashes of neon pink, green, and blue.

posts.caption:
#Opinion | Standup comedian Kaneez Surka @kaneezsurka recently opened up about the "beauty of divorce" on a podcast by Aditi Chandanani @therapywith_aditivc. "Oh you MUST get divorced!" Kaneez quipped, getting Aditi all excited about 'life 2.0'. "When you get divorced, society just drops all expectations of you... they give up on you, and the giving up is the best part!" Kaneez expressed.
Divorce used to be a woman's cue to hide way from society; a label that crept into every introduction. She is just a woman anymore; she is a divorcee. But the women of 2026 are rewriting what it means to walk away from an unfulfilling marriage. They are refusing to carry endings like shame, turning divorce into a cultural statement. Earlier, divorce defined the woman. Now, the woman defines the divorce.
A woman who leaves an unhappy marriage disrupts a comforting myth - that self sacrifice is the highest form of femininity. When she says, “This is not working for me,” she exposes how many others are quietly tolerating what does not work for them either. And that can feel threatening to many.  But here is the truth women know in their bones. Divorce doesn't celebrate heartbreak; it is usually the aftermath of it. So why should women carry the shame of something that often requires immense courage? Why should they be the ones apologising for choosing peace?
Read the full article at www.shethepeople.tv | Views expressed by the author are their own.

posts.caption:
We often witness mothers-in-law bestowing gold jewellery upon their daughters-in-law during weddings, often remarking how the piece is pushtaini (ancestral), once passed down to them by their own mothers-in-law. This tradition of passing down ornaments goes far beyond mere ritual. These heirlooms were originally given with love and care, intended to ensure the financial security of the women in the family. Even decades later, gold and property retain substantial value, safeguarding future generations against financial instability. Generations ago, gold wasn't just ornamental-they were a woman's rainy-day fund, a lifeline in times of uncertainty.
Unlike today, passing down property or gold wasn't a transaction or tool of control-it was a safeguard. According to the Arthashastra, a woman's ownership of Stridhana was legally recognised. Women had full rights to own, manage, and even dispose of their Stridhana, including the freedom to pass it on to their daughters or other relatives. In a society where men were traditionally the primary earners and women managed homes, Stridhana gave women a sense of financial agency and independence.
Before SIPs and stocks, our grandmothers saved in the form of gold. Today, in an age of credit cards, EMIs, and instant gratification, this wisdom remains quietly revolutionary. The most powerful legacy we inherit from the women before us isn't just the gold in our lockers-it's the foresight to prepare for life's uncertainties with grit and unwavering self-worth.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read the full article by @iamkhushidwivedi | Views expressed by the author are their own.
All images are used for representation only.

posts.caption:
As we gear up to visit the big screens when The Devil Wears Prada returns with its sequel, many of us may be turning on our smaller screens and revisiting the original. Not to mention, the various forms of marketing, including an iconic Vogue cover featuring a crossover between Anna Wintour and the equally remarkable Meryl Streep, have only further piqued our interest.
However, an irritating question keeps on returning - how true is the movie and its portrayal of its muse Anna Wintour? So here are some moments from the movie that reveal the truth about the fashion industry (and hopefully harmless lies).
Undoubtedly, the most accurate part of the movie was Miranda Priestly’s office. Why, you may ask? It’s because the production designer actually managed to sneak into Anna Wintour's Condé Nast office to capture a few pictures for reference.
Additionally, Miranda's tension-simmering entrance scene is also said to have held a tinge of truth. While it’s exaggerated for cinematic effect, it is said that Anna Wintour's driver does inform with a call that she has been dropped off at the office. While the staff doesn’t “gird their loins”, they do prepare themselves to present their best during the 2-5 minutes that Anna usually takes to reach the office.
However, one of the first moments we witness of Miranda Priestly’s demeanour towards her employees has allegedly turned out to be false. The interview where Anne Hathaway’s Andy Sachs is so quickly dismissed could’ve been a product of dramatisation. According to former assistants, the interview focuses more on personal interests and questions, not as direct or intimidating.
Read full article by Jai Shah @the_haute_historian  at www.shethepeople.tv
anna wintour vogue the devil wears prada 2 meryl streep miranda priestly andrea andy sachs anne hathaway

Fashion styling has a rate. It always has. The problem is that this industry has never been transparent about what that rate is, and for decades stylists have absorbed the consequences of that silence.
The work starts before the shoot. The expertise takes years to build. The pressure is carried quietly so nobody else has to feel it. And when that work generates commercial value for a brand, a client, or a campaign, it is not a favour. It is a job. And jobs have rates.
We polled over 1,200 stylists across every sector of the industry because we believe that access to this information should not be a privilege. It should be the starting point.
If you are a stylist who has ever quoted blind, undercharged because you were not sure, or struggled to explain your worth to a client, this is for you.
If you are a client, a brand, or someone who has ever wondered what a fashion stylist actually charges and why, this is for you too.

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.

posts.caption:
Jonathan Anderson just showed his first couture collection for Dior. If you do not understand why it mattered, this post is for you.
John Galliano was front row. The artisans were the opening act. The silhouettes came from ceramicist Magdalene Odundo.
If you are in Paris: the show space at Musée Rodin opens to the public from 28 January for one week. The new couture collection, archival Dior pieces, and the Odundo ceramics that inspired it. You can see it yourself.
This is not about liking or not liking a collection. This is about understanding what you are looking at.
The Studio of Stylist Elixir is our online membership. Guides. Templates. Live calls. A community of stylists who are learning and building together. Comment ‘studio’ for a free trial.
Save this. Share it with someone who needs it.
#dior #jonathananderson #stylistelixir
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on January 27, 2026. May be an image of magazine and poster.

posts.caption:
There is a version of busy that feels like building a career.
The test shoots. The unpaid editorials. The creative collaborations. The portfolio that keeps growing. The Instagram that looks good. The diary that looks full.
Months pass.
The dream clients are not reaching out. The paid work is not coming. The career is not moving in the direction it should be.
And the stylist cannot understand why. Because they are working constantly.
Here is what I see again and again in the stylists I mentor.
They are pouring almost all their time into building a portfolio. And almost none of it into putting that portfolio in front of the right people.
A beautiful portfolio that nobody sees does not book work. It never has.
And this is not just a beginner problem.
Ten years in, it looks different but it is the same trap. The diary is full. None of it is the right work. Underpaid jobs. Clients who do not respect the rate. Favours that became jobs that never got charged for.
I remember a woman at Purple PR watching me and my assistant running around one day and saying: you two never stop. She meant it as a compliment. It did not feel like one.
I was running on a treadmill. Moving fast. Going nowhere.
Busy happens by accident. Booked happens by intention.
The full piece is on Substack today. It covers the audit I walk every stylist through when they come to me stuck in this pattern. What to look at. What to change. What to stop doing.
Comment SUBSTACK and I will send you the link.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on April 17, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine, poster and text.

posts.caption:
There is a particular kind of quiet that settles into a fashion styling career when things slow down.
It is not dramatic. Nobody announces it. One week the emails are less frequent. Then a little less. Then you are checking your inbox with a kind of hope that starts to feel embarrassing.
And the whole time you are telling yourself it is temporary. The industry is slow right now. The right people just have not seen the work yet. Something will come.
Most stylists I know have lived inside that quiet at some point. Some of them are living inside it right now.
The hardest thing about it is not the silence itself. It is the story the silence tells you about yourself. That you are not good enough. Not connected enough. Not visible enough.
Here is what I know after years of mentoring stylists.
Visibility in this industry is not something that happens to you. It is something you build. Slowly. Unglamorously. Often without anyone noticing for a while.
And the decision to start building it is the hardest part. Not because it is complicated. Because it requires going first before you feel ready.
This week on the Stylist Elixir Substack I wrote about exactly that. The three things that are actually stopping you from getting booked and the one decision that changes everything.
It is completely free to read.
Comment SEEN below and I will send you the link straight to your DMs. 💚

posts.caption:
The hardest thing about building a fashion styling career is not the work.
It is comparing yourself to someone who started with connections, money, and a head start.
The industry is easier to break into when you already have all of that. That is just true.
But it has never only been for them.
Some of the most interesting careers in this industry have been built by people who worked two jobs to fund the test shoot. Who had no contacts and made them from scratch. Who had no head start and created one.
It is harder. It is not impossible.
This is what we talk about at Stylist Elixir. 💚

posts.caption:
No one orders the same thing at the table.
Groups arrive with mixed preferences, generations, dietary needs and uneven familiarity with cuisines. One menu holds all of it. That’s where Pan Asian restaurants come in. A single menu spanning multiple Asian cuisines.
Chinese cuisine anchors that experience. It has been around the longest and has adapted to Indian tastes, making it easier to introduce Japanese, Korean, Thai or Vietnamese dishes into the same meal.
Even as single-cuisine restaurants grow, Pan Asian continues to work because of how India eats, social, shared and built around variety.
This isn’t new. Head to the link in bio to see why Pan Asian restaurants continue to dominate and what they reveal about eating out in India.
By Karishma Kuenzang ( @kkuenzang )
#PanAsian #DiningTrends #FoodCulture #IndianDining #RestaurantTrends

posts.caption:
ADHD in women often reads as competence: multitasking, perfectionism, emotional labour. Underneath, it’s chronic overwhelm, time blindness, and exhaustion.
Long  framed as a male disorder, ADHD symptoms in women are overlooked, or misread  as stress. The result is years of self-blame before recognition. Masking, hormonal shifts, and the cost of care in India continue to delay diagnosis and limit access to treatment.
The system isn’t missing ADHD in women. It’s been trained not to see it.
Read more at the link in bio
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)
#ADHD #MentalHealthAwareness #WomensHealth #Neurodiversity #Wellness

posts.caption:
Family in India is ritual, inheritance, and belonging. It is also a negotiation between silence, recognition, and exclusion.
Festivals mark who is called home. For many LGBTQ people, that call does not always come. Disinheritance extends beyond wills. It shapes who is remembered, invited, and named.
The cost is emotional and material. Housing, inheritance, and legitimacy still hinge on heteronormative structures.
Yet kinship persists. Chosen families cook, care, and carry traditions forward. They invest in niblings, sustain intergenerational ties, and reshape belonging on their own terms.
Read more at the link in bio.
By Tejaswi Subramanian (@tejnesss)
#QueerIndia #QueerRelationships #ChosenFamily #LGBTQVoices #TheEstablished

posts.caption:
Bodycare products were once an afterthought. Soap bars, shea butter, polite fragrance. That hierarchy is shifting.
Oils are positioned around recovery. Lotions around sleep. Serums draw from Ayurveda. The category is being reframed as ritual.
Consumers are looking beyond moisturised skin—toward confidence, calm, and emotional ease. The expectation has expanded, even as the science remains uneven.
Bodycare products now sit in a grey zone. Compelling, yet still being proven.
Read more at the link in bio.
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)
#BodyCareProducts #WellnessIndustry #IndiaBeauty #BeautyBusiness #ConsumerTrends

posts.caption:
Alia Bhatt (@aliaabhatt) joins Myntra as its ambassador, taking on a role spanning the platform’s largest categories.
Few names operate across fashion and beauty with equal ease. Bhatt’s presence sits comfortably across everyday consumption and global brand ecosystems, making her a natural fit for a platform built on scale and accessibility.
That range matters for a platform like Myntra. It has long led online fashion retail in India, but adjacent categories and higher frequency consumption increasingly shape its growth. It has built its base in fashion, with beauty growing alongside it. Myntra Beauty focuses on making quality-led products more accessible, a shift reflected in its growth.
Myntra doesn’t need a face to introduce it. It has scale. The consumer today moves across high and low, fashion and beauty, all at once. Bhatt fits that behaviour neatly.
She fits seamlessly into how people already shop on Myntra today—fluid, frequent, and across categories. Her alignment sharpens the platform’s core: a consumer journey that moves instinctively between aspiration and access, with no friction in between.

posts.caption:
Ghosting, catfishing, and dating apps fatigue have quietly reshaped modern dating in India.
Singles aren’t swiping in isolation anymore. Friends are stepping in as matchmakers, sounding boards, and red-flag spotters. WhatsApp group chats weigh in, cafés and breweries are hosting mixers again, and even Tinder is experimenting with formats that mirror this shift.
Where parents once played cupid, friends now hold more influence. Emotional compatibility, political alignment, and personality quirks carry more weight than family background or income. Dating has moved from instinct to consultation, from private decisions to something more collective.
Read more at the link in bio
By Karishma Kuenzang (@kkuenzang)
#Groupchatdating #datingculture #GenZIndia #datingapps #groupchat

posts.caption:
New era, same cinematic chaos. Olivia Rodrigo just dropped “drop dead,” the lead single from her upcoming third album, and the visual alone already feels like a moment. Directed by longtime collaborator Petra Collins, the video leans into that dreamy, slightly unhinged aesthetic they’ve perfected since “good 4 u” and “brutal.”
But the real headline? The Palace of Versailles.
Rodrigo is wandering (and dancing) through the palace like it’s her very own—including the Galerie des Batailles and the Queen’s Apartments—making this the first music video ever filmed inside the private royal apartments. Casual.
The contrast is kind of perfect: historic excess meets modern angst, all wrapped in that soft, surreal Petra Collins lens.
#OliviaRodrigo #DropDead #PetraCollins #Versailles #MusicVideo

posts.caption:
Exploitation in the fashion industry is driven by a stark gender imbalance. The numbers are clear—women make up the vast majority of garment workers (80–85%), yet remain systematically underpaid, underprotected, and excluded from positions of power.
The continued exclusion of women from fashion’s executive circles—and their concentration in its most exploited labor segments—cannot be explained by profit alone, but by the persistence of long-standing systems of power.
Read the latest on Substack: The Citizen’s Poste | posteposteposte
#Substack #WomenInFashion #FashionIndustry #FashionEconomics #TheCitizensPoste

posts.caption:
I can’t believe I’m just now catching up on @vogueportugal’s spring content, because this is so so good.
The “Once Upon a Kiss” editorial from the March 2026 Kiss Issue feels like a full fantasy world, not just a shoot. Directed and photographed by @elionogueiraofficial, it reimagines classic fairytales—especially Sleeping Beauty—through a slightly darker, more surreal lens. Think “deadly kisses,” but make it fashion.
What really makes it hit is the set design. Artist @janafrost.art built these insanely detailed, hand-cut paper environments that blur the line between physical and digital. It’s intricate, theatrical, and just a little eerie in the best way.
This is what editorial should feel like right now: immersive, referential, and actually imaginative.

posts.caption:
Did Jane Birkin secretly hate the Birkin? The short answer is no. But she definitely didn’t treat it the way the internet does.
She loved the idea of the bag (it was literally designed for her), but she was also very honest about the reality: it was heavy, a little impractical, and not exactly aligned with her effortless, bohemian style. She famously called it “bloody heavy” and joked it probably gave her shoulder problems.
What’s more interesting is how she used it. She covered her Birkins in stickers, beads, and charms, let them wear down, and often overfilled them. You know, treated them like an actual everyday bag, not a museum piece.
And at one point in 2015, she even asked Hermès to remove her name from the crocodile version over animal welfare concerns, although she later continued her association with the brand.
So no, she didn’t hate it. She just didn’t idolize it. Which, ironically, is exactly what made her style so iconic.
#JaneBirkin #Hermes #BirkinBag #FashionHistory #PersonalStyle

posts.caption:
I don’t enjoy vintage shopping. There. I said it. For all the romanticization of vintage and archival in fashion over the last few years, I just can’t do it. That said, I also live part-time in Paris—widely considered the unofficial capital of vintage shopping.
So this spring, I took it upon myself to get with the program. I’ve tried the hole-in-the-wall spots, the kilo shops, the friend-of-a-friend’s gatekept recommendations, and the TikTok favorites—but these five are the ones that actually deliver. Whether you’re a vintage enthusiast or not, they’re worth the visit.

posts.caption:
Mrs. Nita Ambani honoured the extraordinary artists who brought India’s spirit to life, marking NMACC’s 3rd anniversary. The legendary trio Shankar-Ehsaan-Loy (@shankarehsaanloy) alongside the all-time-favourite Shreya Ghoshal (@shreyaghoshal), Rajasthani folk icon Mame Khan (@mame_khan), Gujarat’s celebrated duo Osman Mir (@osman.mir) & Aamir Mir (@aamir_o_mir) and percussion prodigy Ojas Adhiya (@ojasadhiya), created a soaring tribute to the country’s musical heritage in Ek Sur – Bharat Ki Awaaz at The Grand Theatre(@nmacc.india).
Veteran performer Shekhar Suman (@shekhusuman) celebrated devotional musician Agam Aggarwal(@agam.zen), sitar virtuoso Swayam (@swayam.sitar) and Bharatanatyam luminary Radhe Jaggi (@radhejaggi), who added to the festivities at NMACC’s Studio Theatre and The Cube, were also felicitated as part of the celebrations.
To more experiences that leave us spellbound!
#CultureAtTheCentre #3YearsOfNMACC

posts.caption:
Manoj Bajpayee (@bajpayee.manoj) is ridiculously youthful-looking for a 50-something. He’s trim, his face glows, he’s light on his feet. His body of work is already impressive and curated, and his physical self suggests the same level of care and attention. “You sacrifice a lot and don’t compromise with your daily routine,” says Bajpayee. It’s been 17 years since he started skipping dinners, fitness advice that has now become a habit. His work follows the same rigour.
From the beginning, there’s been a palpable hunger in Bajpayee’s progression—an unwavering journey towards excellence that has been present ever since he became the centrepiece of Ram Gopal Varma’s classic crime drama ‘Satya’ as the flamboyant gangster Bhiku Mhatre in 1998. Over the years, the actor became the name to go to for films and shows that demanded an assiduous unpacking of characters with his ability to not just inhabit a part but also make its impact linger beyond the end credits. Today, he’s at the top of his game, occupying a position that once was the late Irrfan’s, straddling mainstream, indie, and middle-of-the-road projects.
Nila Iyer (@nilaiyer) sits down with Bajpayee to discuss films, family, and his 30-year-long career.

posts.caption:
Manoj Bajpayee’s (@bajpayee.manoj) filmography is a “mixed bag”, he points out—by design. His upcoming projects include ‘Governor’ directed by Chinmay Mandlekar, and a reunion with Ram Gopal Varma for the horror comedy ‘Police Station Mein Bhoot’. Neeraj Pandey’s crime thriller for Netflix is also in the pipeline, as is American director Ben Rekhi’s upcoming adaptation of Arvind Adiga’s novel ‘Last Man in Tower’.
Right now, Bajpayee is at that sweet spot in his career where he can take the long view. On screen, he has played losers and heroes, but IRL he’s able to balance celebrity with craft and Bollywood’s ruthless demands with his individual ambitions. For him, mainstream cinema is a strategic choice. “The Hindi film industry is very market-oriented, so to keep one’s value alive one has to balance it with things on which I don’t compromise,” Bajpayee explains.
Read Nila Iyer’s (@nilaiyer) full interview with the Bollywood actor at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
If one were to draw parallels between ‘The Devil Wears Prada’ and Caroline Palmer’s ‘Workhorse’(@carolinepalmer_workhorse), it would be that both follow a fashion-world outsider trying to break into (and survive) the tightly guarded clique of insiders. But that’s where the similarities end. Where sweet, earnest Andy Sachs was running across NYC traffic trying to procure a ‘Harry Potter’ manuscript for The Twins and getting Miranda’s extremely specific lunch order, Clodagh ‘Clo’ Harmon is lying on her résumé, pilfering petty cash for cigarettes, stealing art from a rich Hamptons house for sport, and sabotaging colleagues for better bylines—all to finally make it in the scary, intimidating world of the ‘Vogue’-esque but unnamed fashion magazine she works at. Where ‘TDWP’ is the glossy, mainstream Hollywood version of that struggle, ‘Workhorse’ is the unhinged A24 take.
At the link in bio, @ridhimasapre chats with Palmer about complicated women, loving the idea of making readers a little uncomfortable, and more.

posts.caption:
If your recent oversized cotton tee from Zara(@zara) has a vibrant artwork—women lounging, glossy desserts, soft chaos, flowing hair—front and centre, you already own a piece of artist Manuja Waldia’s(@manujawaldia) works. It’s the kind you might notice in-store before you realise it’s hers.
The 34-year-old first drew widespread attention with her contemporary Shakespeare covers for Penguin Random House’s Pelican series, where she reimagined the classics through bold, archival-style compositions. Since then, she has collaborated with brands like Sephora, created a Google Doodle honouring Begum Akhtar, and worked on limited-edition prints with Dishoom.
Across her work, you will notice recurring details from everyday Indian life, whether it is familiar objects like oil bottles and mangoes or the way she returns to themes of female friendships and sisterhood, all rendered with colour, care, and a strong sense of memory.
Read @sheyakurian’s full interview with Waldia at the link in bio

posts.caption:
Viya by Vikram Goyal Launches Womenswear
Delhi-based lifestyle brand Viya has debuted its womenswear line, marking a shift from home design into clothing. Under the creative direction of founder Vikram Goyal, the line extends the brand’s material-led aesthetic into silhouettes like kaftans, tunics and overlap sets. The collection draws on plants and sun sign associations in the Astra series and fruit-inspired prints in Blossom, with fabrics like chanderi, ramie and silk. Brass detailing and handcraft techniques anchor the line, now available online, at Viya’s Mumbai store and Ogaan outlets.
Agilitas Signs Smriti Mandhana
Agilitas Sports has signed cricketer Smriti Mandhana as brand ambassador for its sportswear brand ONE8. The move brings Mandhana alongside cricketer Virat Kohli within the ONE8 ecosystem. She will contribute to product development across cricket performance gear and high-intensity training wear. With this appointment, the brand hopes to position women’s sports as a core growth pillar rather than a secondary category.
Highlights from Watches and Wonders 2026
From perfumed straps to the world’s thinnest dial, international trade show Watches and Wonders stands out for showcasing horology innovations. At its ongoing edition in Geneva, luxury watchmaker Panerai unveiled Luminor 31 Giorni, the world’s first watch with a 31-day power reserve—a piece that was seven years in the making. Other highlights include Piaget’s ‘Swinging Pebbles’ collection, which marks a return to its jewelled watches first launched in 1969. Fit to be worn as a necklace, it includes a bejewelled dial featuring stones like tiger’s eye, verdite and pietersite.
Stella McCartney for H&M
High street clothing label H&M has unveiled its collaboration with British designer Stella McCartney. With a campaign fronted by models Reneé Rapp, Adwoa Aboah, and Angelina Kendall, the lineup includes tailored suiting, slouchy trenches, bedazzled tops and colourful playsuits. Produced consciously using organic cotton and recycled glass, this is McCartney’s sophomore collaboration with the fast fashion giant that has collaborated with ERDEM and Balmain in the past.

posts.caption:
In the past few weeks, while researching vegan fashion, I was reminded that the Victoria’s Secret Fashion Show 2025, that returned after a six-year hiatus, had announced a new era of “Angels”. The casting had changed. Inclusivity was the headline. Angel Reese, the American baseball player walked the runway along with
fashion’s famously “perfect” bodies. The wings were no longer real feathers. But the word “Angel”? Or for that matter, “bronzed goddess”, used to describe Reese in the brand’s most recent campaign? Gendered. The body in fashion has certainly diversified. But old metaphors stick.
Virgin Mary, the mocktail. Chick flick, the film. Drama queen, the taunt. None of these sound particularly offensive. That is precisely the point.
Because casual sexism does not directly fly into our eyes. But, at a time when we are learning to correct language—rethinking descriptors
for disability, refraining from calling people blind or fat, respecting pronouns, and learning to rebalance credit and earnings between artisans, makers and
designers—gender in everyday vocabulary slips through unexamined. Virgin Mary may just be a drink, but it borrows from the idea of being untainted
or “pure”. The absence of alcohol is feminised. A chick flick reduces an entire genre before it is even watched; the audience is presumed. And drama queen—despite its reinventions among queer performers—retains its original charge: that visible emotion is indulgent and excessive, it should be either corrected or laughed off.
There is no moral onus on lifestyle industries to strip speech of every gendered metaphor. Language would collapse under that kind of policing. But sometimes
we might have a cultural obligation—at least to notice what we repeat and why. Since fashion has updated the optics while forgetting to edit the script.
🖋️@shefatwork

Textile artist Ekta Kaul’s book 𝑲𝒂𝒏𝒕𝒉𝒂: 𝑺𝒖𝒔𝒕𝒂𝒊𝒏𝒂𝒃𝒍𝒆 𝑻𝒆𝒙𝒕𝒊𝒍𝒆𝒔 𝒂𝒏𝒅 𝑴𝒊𝒏𝒅𝒇𝒖𝒍 𝑴𝒂𝒌𝒊𝒏𝒈 journeys across continents, documenting the 𝒌𝒂𝒏𝒕𝒉𝒂𝒔 housed in museums, private collections and the homes of Bengali women keeping the craft vibrant even today. From uncovering a surprising connection between 𝒌𝒂𝒏𝒕𝒉𝒂 motifs and the Bengali tradition of 𝒂𝒍𝒑𝒂𝒏𝒂, to discovering the geometric designs from artisans in Murshidabad, and speaking to modern-day practitioners of the craft, Kaul’s book treats 𝒌𝒂𝒏𝒕𝒉𝒂 as an alive, evolving and approachable art form. Link in bio.

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com

posts.caption:
What’s interesting is that the critique being made here can also be applied to the work being produced.
Looking at these sewing patterns you sell, Cordelia, Audrey, Odette, Sleeve Studio, we’re not seeing something created from nothing. We’re seeing reinterpretations of existing garment archetypes: gathered waist dresses, puff and balloon sleeves, pleated skirts, 1930s–1950s silhouettes, romantic and vintage-inspired constructions.
These are established design vocabularies that have existed for decades, sometimes centuries.
And that’s not a criticism.
That’s exactly how fashion works.
These patterns are: adaptationsc, recompositions, translations of historical references into contemporary, accessible forms.
They draw from a shared visual and technical heritage, and reframe it for a new audience. No one would call that theft. We call it design.
And this is precisely where the earlier argument becomes inconsistent.
Because what is described as “extraction” or “appropriation” in the case of Art Deco is, in practice, not so different in structure from what any designer, including pattern makers, does: You observe, you study, you absorb, you reinterpret.
Art Deco operated in the same way: drawing from Egyptian, Persian, Japanese, and other visual languages, abstracting them, transforming them into a new modern aesthetic.
Of course, historical context matters. Power dynamics matter. And yes, exploitation especially in production remains a serious issue.
But to conflate influence with erasure, or reinterpretation with theft, creates a contradiction.
Because if we follow that logic strictly, then no design practice remains untouched.
Not even pattern making.
Fashion has never been about creating in isolation.
It is a continuous process of reference, transformation, and reinvention.
The question is not whether influence exists it always has.
The question is how consciously, respectfully, and intelligently it is handled.
And that requires nuance.

posts.caption:
As many of you, I have recently finished watching The Other Bennet Sister and I cannot stop thinking about it.
I immediately felt this protectiveness over Mary and what her character had to deal with in her family. In this adaptation we see a far crueler version of Mrs Bennet and how Mary’s experience differed so greatly from that of her sisters’.
Most adaptations have made Mary the joke. The plain one, the odd one, the embarrassment. This version reveals the vivid, multidimensional personality of Mary. Her brilliant mind, her quick wit and how she learns to put herself first after a lifetime of putting herself last.
I highly recommend this show. I laughed, I cried and I felt deeply for Mary and her arc in this story.
Well done to @ellabruccoleri for portraying Mary’s depth, kindness and unapologetic fashion sense.
And to @sianjenkinscostume for such beautiful costumes! I personally would happily wear EVERYTHING Mrs. Gardiner wore, what a colour palette.

posts.caption: Cortisol—known as the “stress hormone”—is produced by the adrenal glands as a response to stressful or dangerous situations. Regulating immune response, blood sugar, and blood pressure, the hormone is essential for our survival. However, high levels of cortisol can affect our lymphatic system and cause puffiness in the eyes, cheeks, and jaw. Get a head start at mitigating stress in your daily life and tap the link in our bio for 15 foods that naturally lower cortisol levels.

posts.caption: There is 100% a need for a store like Barney’s in the US retail ecosystem right now but make no mistake - the world is a different place than it was when it last existed. A new Barney’s needs to over index on discovery (what made them famous to begin with) and activation. It needs to be a true experience for guests. The brand curation needs to be one of a kind (LVMH brands have their own boutiques everywhere so we don’t need Barney’s to get that stuff). The team needs to curate brands from around the world - stuff you can’t get everywhere. It needs to be a launch point for the best young brands that everyone will covet a few years from now (working w young brands requires its own strategy but it will be worth it). It needs to build in flexible space for creative activation that changes frequently to create a reason to come in early and often. Fragrance curation is a huge opportunity. An Asia-style food hall (along with Fred’s) will make the store a place for regular gathering and socializing. It’s really something that has evaded the US department stores today. If they can pull it off, there’s never been a better time for a Barney’s revival.

posts.caption:
What happens when you put Meryl Streep and Anna Wintour—the two Mirandas, as it were—in a room together? A wide-ranging conversation about fashion, family, friendship, and yes, the forthcoming sequel to “The Devil Wears Prada.”
“In terms of Miranda, and coming back to that character 20 years later, I did think honestly about Anna, and tried to imagine what it was like to carry her responsibility and to be as interested in the world and curious as she must have to be,” Streep tells Greta Gerwig in Vogue’s May 2026 cover story. And what did Wintour do when she heard the rumours that “The Devil Wears Prada 2” was happening? She called Streep, of course. “I knew she would tell me if it was going to be all right,” Wintour says. “I trusted her implicitly.”
Read the full story at the link in bio.

A paragon of pared-back style– beloved by the likes of Kate Moss and Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy in its heyday–the minimalist midi is a low-effort, high-impact summer wardrobe staple. posts.caption: Hayden Anhedönia—the singer behind the Ethel Cain persona—tapped her friend Jonathan Anderson to create a pair of bespoke overalls for her Weekend 1 performance at #Coachella. But for Weekend 2, Anhedönia wanted to embrace the other side of @mothercain. @Dior fashioned Anhedönia a black mock-neck gown with a gathered waist and flowing sleeves affixed with organza petals. “The dress feels like it fits right into the original quintessential Ethel Cain funereal garb, less narrative and more abject conceptual,” she says. See more at the link in our bio. Photos: @enochchuangphoto and @advli
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Vogue on April 19, 2026 tagging @enochchuangphoto. May be an image of sable coat and text.

posts.caption: The Tiffany & Co. Blue Book gala was the soirée to attend this week—and @roses_are_rosie had to be there. “The more and more I get into fashion and dressing myself, I think I’m gravitating towards classic and timeless pieces,” the star says of her latest glamorous look. See inside Rosé’s evening at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue on April 18, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, blonde hair, makeup, dress and text.

posts.caption: Congratulations to the newlyweds, @Annaa.Hall and Darius Slayton (@sl1msl8y)! The professional track star, Olympian, and reigning heptathlon world champion and New York Giants wide receiver exchanged vows on April 11 at Oheka Castle on Long Island. “I had seen her running on TV, and when she came across the screen, I thought she was pretty, so I followed her on Instagram,” recalls Darius—as one does in this day and age. After a little over a year of dating, they got engaged in November of 2025, at the stadium where they met. Their wedding took place five months later in a historic jewel of a mansion located on Long Island’s Gold Coast. Tap the link in our bio to see more photos from the couple's special day. Photos: @magifisher
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue on April 17, 2026. May be a black-and-white image of wedding and text.

posts.caption: When a young Daniella Kallmeyer attended a summer dance intensive at the Martha Graham School in New York little did she know that years later she would be transforming one of her dress designs into costumes for the same stage. Last night at the Martha Graham Dance Company’s 100th Anniversary Gala, dancers Xin Ying and Lloyd Knight wore two custom @Kallmeyerofficial creations to perform a special excerpt from O Thou Desire Who Art About to Sing at the New York Public Library. Learn more about the full circle moment at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on April 18, 2026. May be an image of dancing and text.

posts.caption: After a brief a 19-month tenure at Gucci, @Sabato.DeSarno has shifted his attention away from the runway’s glare and into the more forgiving, and arguably more interesting, worlds of art and design. This year’s Salone del Mobile sees him stepping back into the conversation with “Insieme” (“Together” in Italian), an ambitious project about the knowledge and beauty behind the handcrafted. At the link in , De Sarno  talks about his life after Gucci, the social media backlash, his new projects, and why he thinks ready-to-wear isn’t exactly in great shape right now with @tiziana_cardini.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on April 17, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, hat, magazine and text that says 'VOGUE RUNWAY M SABATO DE SARNO PUTS ON HIS CURATOR'S HAT FOR A NEW PROJECT AHEAD OF SALONE DEL MOBILE'.

posts.caption:
“It was a risk,” says @StellaMcCartney, recalling the decision to launch her collaboration with @HM in 2005. A risk that paid off: Looking back at the headlines from the time, the release of McCartney’s collection sparked an unprecedented frenzy. So it feels like a full-circle moment for the designer that now, just over 20 years later, she’s dropping a new collection on May 7 with the Swedish retail powerhouse—one that feels almost like a miniature retrospective of her greatest hits over the intervening years. “I really wanted to revisit the Stella wardrobe, the icons,” says McCartney of the collection.
At the link in bio, the designer shares a first look at her new H&M collection—which has been almost entirely designed using organic, recycled, and next-gen sustainable fabrics—with Vogue.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on April 15, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, magazine and text that says 'VOGUE RUNWAY STELLA MCCARTNEY ON RETURNING TO COLLABORATE WITH H&M, 20 YEARS LATER'.

posts.caption:
Is fashion art? Is art fashion? The answer to those perennial, pesky questions should be resolved this May when The Metropolitan Museum of Art’s Costume Institute, after years in the basement, expands upstairs into prime real estate, just adjacent to the @metmuseum’s Great Hall. “In a way fashion is beyond art,” says the Costume Institute’s curator in charge, Andrew Bolton. “It embodies our lived experience. It’s the only art form that does that.” Ahead of the 2026 #MetGala, head to the link in our bio for a sneak peek inside the @metcostumeinstitute’s upcoming exhibition, “Costume Art.”
Photographed by @ethanjamesgreen, Written by Dodie Kazanjian, Styled by @amandaharlech, Hair by @jimmypaulhair, Makeup by @kabukinyc, Tailoring by @carol_ai_studio, Manicures by @jinsoonchoi, Set Design by @frau.juliawagner, Produced by @specialproduction.agency. Vogue, May 2026.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on April 15, 2026. May be an image of dancing and text.

posts.caption: Over the years, the Wessel & Vett prize has bolstered the success of Scandi brands including Cecilie Bahnsen, Saks Potts, and Anne Sofie Madsen. Nicklas Skovgaard took home the prize in 2025. The makeup of this year’s finalists is representative of where fashion is going globally. Berner Kühl and O. Files represent the continuing industry excitement in menswear. While all of these Danish brands work with sustainability, responsible design is central to Bonnetje’s practice. Caro Editions, meanwhile, leans into vintage and has a playfulness that’s stamped with the personality of its founder, the former model Caroline Bille Brahe. Learn more about the 2026 finalists at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on April 14, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'VOGUE RUNWAY GET TO KNOW THE DANISH BRANDS COMPETING FOR SCANDINAVIA'S BIGGEST FASHION PRIZE'.

posts.caption: A runway show is a multisensory experience, in which sound, music, casting, set design, and inspirations come alive. posts.caption: We all have that one friend we turn to for fashion advice. On the internet, that second set of eyes is @LaurelPantin. For her very first #AskEarl, she’s talking about color. This season’s runways were full of bold, primary colors from designers like Loewe, Celine, and (her personal favorite) Dries van Noten. While the trend can feel intimidating, Laurel keeps it simple: “Trusting your instincts is a great way to discover what your personal style actually is.” See how she styles the trend and shop each look at the link in bio.

posts.caption: The enduring pull of ’90s fashion lies in its simplicity. While trends come and go, the decade’s signature silhouettes have never really gone out of style. Among the great ’90s staples that have retained their cultural capital today are the slip dress, the cigarette jean, and the pencil skirt. Shop Vogue's edit of the decade’s defining pieces that still hold up at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
There is no looking away. Not from the magnetic force that is @beccca. Whilst it was her breakout role in 'Gap: The Series' that brought her global fame, her staying impact feels relentless. And it's one that starts to make sense when studying her serious credentials across acting, singing, and modelling.
"Each discipline fulfils a different creative side of me. Acting allows me to tell stories, music feels very personal, and modelling connects me with fashion and visual expression," shares #RebeccaArmstrong, who joins us on our April 'Retrofuture' issue as one of two cover stars.
In the link in bio, we delve into the new creative challenges she's taking on in 2026, and what she's been learning about herself along the way.

posts.caption:
@caileespaeny is in between worlds. Having played characters that span across time and space, the Golden Globe-nominated actress has a fascinating plurality about her that feels both familiar and fresh.
Cast in a nostalgic mood that calls to mind the filmography of #DavidLynch and #StanleyKubrick, the actress transforms into a defiant, enigmatic persona as our 'Retrofuture' cover star, lensed by the legendary Canadian photographer @normanjeanroy. "For the shoot, we were tapping into a bit of a David Lynch world. It was so fun to lock into this set and character with the team on this shoot," Spaeny muses.
As one of two cover stars for April's 'Retrofuture' issue, Spaeny lets us into her universe—from her determined portrayal of Rain Carradine in 'Alien: Romulus' to the tender portrait she paints in 'Priscilla' and her newest work for @netflix's limited series 'Beef'. The full cover story, now in the link in bio.

posts.caption:
There is a certain joy when one lands the most exciting vintage finds. That moment of sheer bliss when you pick up that rare item you’ve been trawling the internet for. Now, it’s time to drum up that excitement, with #VogueSingapore’s first-ever Vogue Vintage Market.
Presented with Southeast Asia’s leading auction house @hotlotz, the inaugural market will feature over 500 different pieces of clothing, costume jewellery, accessories and more. Specially curated by the Vogue Singapore team, the selection will include rare archival pieces, as well as items from the personal collections of editor-in-chief @monkiepoo and other local fashion collectors, with part of the proceeds going to the #VogueFoundation.
Taking place on 16 April, Vogue Vintage is open to the public, and Club Vogue members will receive exclusive early access and discounts on items at our first vintage market—sign up now in the link in bio to reserve a spot.

posts.caption:
✨ Creator, Actor, PHENOMENON! ✨ On the June-July cover of Wedding Vows, we present the trailblazing @mostlysane, Prajakta Koli!
From building a YouTube empire to being the lead for Netflix Original Mismatched, from being part of Michelle Obama’s Day Time Emmy winning docu-series to being UNDP India's First Youth Climate Champion, her journey is nothing short of extraordinary.
With her debut romance novel set to launch this year, along with her first Amazon Prime Original Andhera, Prajakta’s journey is a testament to the power of dreams, hard work, and staying true to oneself. She continues to inspire millions with her authenticity, relatability, and unstoppable spirit.

posts.caption:
The Great Gatsby. Promising Young Woman. Maestro. There’s no shortage of career highlights for Carey Mulligan. Our April cover star has never shied away from portraying complicated women with rich inner lives, and now, she’s stepping into her messiest role yet. “I was like, ‘Oh, that’s insane and feral. And I loved it,’” Mulligan recalls of reading one particularly unhinged scene in season 2 of Beef, the Emmy-winning dark comedy-drama miniseries in which her character’s marriage devolves into resentment, blackmail, and regret. With the sophomore season hitting Netflix this month and Greta Gerwig’s highly anticipated Narnia adaptation coming later this year, the actress has her hands full with one buzzy project after another. From Mulligan’s real marriage advice to her dream projects, go deeper into our April cover story at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
@chanelofficial unveils the ‘Coco Game’ collection at Watches & Wonders 2026 in Geneva a playful yet precise fusion of haute horology and house mythology.
At its core sits a one-of-a-kind chess set, where every piece is reimagined through Chanel’s codes. The Vendôme column stands as the rook, the lion rules as king, and sculpted couture busts take the place of bishops. Gabrielle Chanel herself becomes the queen her time discreetly concealed beneath her iconic slingbacks.
The board is crafted in white gold and black ceramic, a sharp contrast of light and shadow. Each queen is adorned in diamond tweed a setting engineered exclusively for this creation and can be worn as a pendant on a white gold, diamond, and onyx chain. Unsurprisingly, the piece is already spoken for.
Beyond the centrepiece, the ‘Coco Game’ narrative extends across Chanel’s watchmaking icons. On the J12, Mademoiselle appears as a pixelated figure marking the seconds, precision-cut from carbon plate. On the Boy·Friend, she takes form as the Queen of Hearts, elegantly poised on the dial.
📸 : Chanel
posts.alt_text: Photo by WOMENCULTURE on April 15, 2026. May be an image of magazine, poster and text that says 'FASHION Chanel Turned Its Icons Into A Chess Set For "Coco Game" WOMEn CULTURE CUL JRE'.

posts.caption:
The @tiffanyandco Blue Book Collection 2026, titled Hidden Garden, is a bold return to nature reimagined through high jewelry at its most artistic. Inspired by the legendary Jean Schlumberger, the collection transforms birds, butterflies, and blossoms into surreal, gem-set sculptures.
Under the direction of Nathalie Verdeille, each piece feels alive featuring rare gemstones, vibrant color pairings, and intricate craftsmanship that pushes beyond traditional luxury. Standouts like the reimagined “Bird on a Rock” and fantastical insect motifs blur the line between jewelry and art.
This isn’t just a collection it’s Tiffany turning nature into power, fantasy, and legacy in its purest high-jewelry form.
📸: @tiffanyandco

posts.caption:
After his monumental first @coachella set, Taemin is setting an even higher bar for himself heading into the second weekend of the festival.
“I know that our cultures are different and languages are different, but I’m hoping that this is a really good opportunity for them to meet me as the artist that I am," he told WWD.
Known as the “Idol’s Idol,” Taemin has established his legacy through strong stage performances and choreography, both as a solo artist and with second-generation K-pop group @shinee. He is the first Korean solo male K-pop artist to be featured on the Coachella lineup.
Read the interview at WWD.com — #linkinbio
Report: @ktauer
Photos: @dandoperalski / WWD

posts.caption:
Who is ready for some @madonna?
The super star just teased the artwork for her next album, "Confessions II," set to release this July.
This will be the fifteenth studio album for the “Queen of Pop,” and the sequel to Confessions — the disco, dance-pop album that gave everyone a reason to hit the dance floor back in 2005.
Take a look at Madonna from the Fairchild Archive.
